2015 · CVPR 2015 · Benchmarks & Evals

Deep Neural Networks are Easily Fooled: High Confidence Predictions for Unrecognizable Images

Evolutionary search and gradient ascent can synthesize pure static, stripes, and swirls that a state-of-the-art ImageNet or MNIST classifier will label — with up to 99.99% confidence — as a robin, a king penguin, or a familiar digit, revealing that a deep network's softmax "confidence" is no guarantee it has recognized anything a human would call the target class.

♪ listen — audio episode
3-voice deep dive · mp3 ↗ · rss feed ↗
Anh Nguyen, Jason Yosinski, Jeff Clune
venue CVPR 2015 citations 3,542 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-07-19
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By late 2014, convolutional networks trained on ImageNet were closing in on human-level classification accuracy, and questions naturally turned to whether DNN "vision" resembles human vision in more than just the accuracy number. A contemporaneous result (Szegedy et al., 2013's "Intriguing properties of neural networks") had already shown one alarming gap: an imperceptible perturbation to a correctly-classified photo — say, of a lion — could flip a DNN's label to something else entirely (a library), with the input looking unchanged to a human. That result still starts from a real, recognizable image and nudges it a tiny amount.

This paper asks a more basic and more radical question: is a DNN's confidence tied to recognizability at all? If you build an image completely from scratch — no natural starting point, no constraint that it look like anything — and optimize purely for one number (the network's own predicted probability for a chosen class), how high can that number go, and what does the resulting image look like? If the answer turns out to be "arbitrarily high, and the image looks like nothing," that is a direct problem for every evaluation practice that treats softmax output as a proxy for "the network recognized this." It is the empirical seed of a question the field would spend the next decade trying to answer: what does a deep classifier's confidence actually measure, and can it be trusted as a signal at all — for in-distribution correctness, let alone for detecting when an input doesn't belong to any trained class?

// how it works

The method

The authors test two trained, publicly-available convnets from the Caffe software package: an ImageNet DNN — a reproduction of Krizhevsky et al.'s 2012 "AlexNet," scoring 42.6% top-1 error (close to Krizhevsky's reported 40.7%) on the 1.3M-image, 1,000-class ILSVRC 2012 dataset — and an MNIST DNN, a ReLU variant of LeCun's LeNet scoring 0.94% error (vs. LeNet-5's 0.8%) on handwritten digits. Neither network is modified in any way; both are treated as fixed, white-box fitness functions to be optimized against.

Three independent image-generation methods are used, all driving the same objective:

fitness(image) = softmax(DNN(image))target class // the only signal that drives the search — nothing in the objective rewards looking natural
  1. Direct-encoding evolutionary algorithm. Each image genome is one grayscale integer per pixel (28×28 for MNIST) or three HSV integers per pixel (256×256 for ImageNet), initialized as uniform random noise and mutated pixel-by-pixel (mutation rate starts at 10%/pixel, halving every 1,000 generations). Rather than evolve one image per class, the authors use MAP-Elites — a single run that simultaneously keeps a per-class "champion" image for all 1,000 ImageNet classes (or all 10 MNIST digits) at once, replacing a class's champion whenever a mutant scores higher for that class.
  2. Indirect/CPPN encoding. Images are instead generated by an evolved compositional pattern-producing network (CPPN) that maps each pixel's (x, y) coordinate to a color value, with per-node activation functions (sine, sigmoid, Gaussian, linear) evolved alongside the network's topology and weights. Because a compact CPPN genome affects many pixels at once, CPPN images tend to be regular and symmetric (a Gaussian on x gives left-right symmetry; a sine on y gives vertical repetition) — the same encoding that, on the crowd-sourced site PicBreeder.org, has separately produced recognizable art.
  3. Gradient ascent in pixel space (replicating Simonyan et al., 2013): starting from the ImageNet mean image plus a little noise, backprop the gradient of a chosen class's softmax unit with respect to the input pixels and ascend it directly — no population, no evolution, just optimization.
// what they found

The results

On MNIST, both encodings fool the network almost immediately: in fewer than 50 generations, every evolutionary run produces images the MNIST DNN labels with ≥99.99% confidence as digits 0–9, and by 200 generations the median confidence across all ten digit classes is 99.99% — yet the direct-encoding images (Fig. 4) look like colored static, nothing like handwritten digits. The CPPN-encoded images (Fig. 5) reach the same 99.99% median but contain more regular strokes, and specific patterns recur systematically (images classified as "1" tend to have vertical bars; images classified as "2" tend to have a horizontal bar in the lower half) — evidence the search is exploiting real discriminative features the digit classifier learned, not gaming an arbitrary bug.

ImageNet is harder for direct encoding: even after 20,000 generations, median confidence across all 1,000 classes reaches only 21.59% — but 45 of the 1,000 classes still get pushed to ≥99% confidence (the top two rows of the gallery below). CPPN encoding is dramatically more effective: after just 5,000 generations — a quarter of the compute — median confidence reaches 88.11%, significantly higher than direct encoding's 21.59% (p<0.0001, Mann-Whitney U), and images at ≥99.99% confidence appear across most of the 1,000 classes.

A hand-picked sample chosen to showcase diversity across five CPPN runs averaged 99.12% confidence — and manually deleting the repeated pattern elements common in CPPN images measurably lowered their scores in many cases, indicating the repetition is actively read as discriminative evidence, not incidental to "regularity." The authors are careful to note the flip side too: some CPPN images, shown below with a bar chart of the DNN's top-3 predicted classes, do share real visual features with their target class — a parrot's rainbow plumage, a coffee pot's silhouette, a race car's motion-blur streaks.

SettingMedian confidenceGenerations
MNIST · direct encoding99.99%200
MNIST · CPPN encoding99.99%200
ImageNet · direct encoding21.59%20,000
ImageNet · CPPN encoding88.11%5,000

Fooling also generalizes across networks: CPPN images optimized against one trained DNN reached ≥99.99% confidence on a second, independently-initialized (or differently-architected) DNN trained on the same data — so at least part of what's exploited is a systemic property of the model family, not one random seed's idiosyncrasy. Retraining as a defense had mixed results. Adding fooling images as an explicit "none of the above" (n+1) class and repeating for 15 rounds did not close the MNIST hole — new 99.99%-confidence fooling images kept appearing every round, even once the 11th retrained network's negative class held 25% of its entire training set. On ImageNet, retraining helped more: median confidence for newly-evolved images against the retrained network dropped from 88.1% to 11.7% (p<0.0001) — though that retrained network also flagged 45 of 70 (64%) held-out, human-recognizable CPPN images from PicBreeder as "CPPN-like" on sight, suggesting it learned a generic CPPN-texture cue rather than a deep fix. A third, independent method — gradient ascent, replicating Simonyan et al.'s visualization technique but optimizing for confidence rather than recognizability — also reached 99.99% confidence on images the authors describe as "mostly unrecognizable," showing the phenomenon isn't an artifact of evolutionary search specifically. The authors are careful to note the flip side too: some CPPN images (a baseball, a matchstick, a ping-pong ball, sunglasses) really do resemble their target class once you know the label — a set of these was even accepted into a University of Wyoming art exhibition.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

This is a landmark negative result — a diagnostic demonstration, not a proposed fix or a new scoring method — and it should be read that way. It is easy to conflate with the contemporaneous, more famous line of adversarial-perturbation work (Szegedy et al. 2013, and Goodfellow et al.'s FGSM paper published the same month, December 2014) that this paper itself cites, but the two are conceptually distinct: adversarial perturbation nudges a real, already-correctly-classified image by an imperceptible amount, while this paper builds images from nothing, with zero constraint that the result look natural at all. Both expose that softmax confidence is fragile, but from opposite directions — and together they seeded the OOD-detection and calibration literature (MSP, ODIN, Mahalanobis distance, energy-based scores, Outlier Exposure) that spent years afterward trying to build a confidence signal that doesn't have this failure mode.

baselines

Only two single-run, mid-2014 architectures are tested (a Caffe AlexNet clone, a Caffe LeNet variant) — no ensembles, no heavier-regularized or more modern architectures, no comparison against contemporaneous calibration techniques. Whether the phenomenon fades with scale, capacity, or training recipe is left open.

contamination

Not applicable in the usual train/test-leakage sense — there is no held-out benchmark here. Every "test image" is synthesized on the fly with full white-box access to the very network being attacked, which is the paper's whole point, but it also means the reported numbers are upper bounds under full gradient/fitness access, not a measurement of how often an unconstrained or black-box search would stumble onto a fooling image.

what's missed

The paper measures whether confidence can be pushed arbitrarily high, not how often a deployed system would encounter such an input unprompted, so it doesn't quantify real-world attack surface. It also reports only median confidence per setting rather than a full confidence distribution, an explicit false-positive rate at a chosen threshold, or a calibration metric like ECE — the modern OOD-detection literature's now-standard yardsticks postdate this paper.

would raise confidence

A same-vintage comparison against confidence-calibration methods (temperature scaling, MC-dropout, deep ensembles); testing whether standard adversarial training — the fix Goodfellow's contemporaneous paper proposes for the perturbation attack — also closes this direct-construction gap; and a query-limited or transfer-only replication to bound exploitability without full white-box access.

// bottom line

The result holds up as exactly what it claims to be: proof that a DNN's softmax output, by itself, is not evidence the network has recognized anything a human would recognize. It doesn't propose a defense, and its own retraining experiment shows the naive defense (label fooling images as a new negative class) doesn't reliably work. Its lasting contribution is diagnostic — it is the paper the entire overconfidence-and-OOD-detection research program cites first when motivating why softmax confidence needs a better substitute.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Softmax "confidence" is not proof of recognition: evolutionary search and gradient ascent can drive it to ≥99.99% on images sharing no visual content with the target class, across the large majority of ImageNet's 1,000 classes.
  2. Two structurally different image generators — unconstrained per-pixel noise and regular, symmetric CPPN patterns — plus a third method, gradient ascent, all independently find fooling images, so this is a property of the discriminative decision boundary in high-dimensional space, not a quirk of one search technique.
  3. Retraining with fooling images as an explicit negative class barely helped on MNIST (still 99.99% confidence after 15 rounds) and only partly helped on ImageNet (88.1% → 11.7%) — the naive fix is not reliable.
  4. This is the diagnostic seed of the entire overconfidence/OOD-detection research thread that followed: every later paper proposing a better confidence score (MSP, ODIN, Mahalanobis, energy-based OOD, Outlier Exposure) is directly answering the problem this paper first demonstrated.
